title: DuelCache: Bias-Corrected Counterfactual Policy Ranking for One Shared Linux Page Cache
description: DuelCache argues for a narrower contribution than adaptive policy selection: in programmable Linux page cache, inactive eviction policies are observed only through biased counterfactual shadows induced by the currently active shared cache. It uses a small sentinel region to estimate and correct that ranking bias before switching policies, and it succeeds only if this bias correction beats both an uncalibrated SOPA-style selector and a direct leader-set baseline under the same state budget.
motivation: Programmable Linux page-cache substrates such as cache_ext and PageFlex make multiple eviction policies deployable, but prior adaptive systems like ACME and SOPA already cover policy selection itself. The actual open problem is whether one live shared Linux page cache makes sampled counterfactual ranking inaccurate enough that explicit bias correction is needed, and whether that correction transfers beyond a tiny monitored region under tenant interference and phase changes.
proposed_approach: Maintain one active policy and sampled inactive shadows for a small label-free portfolio (`LRU`, `MRU`, `LFU-aging`, `LHD`). Reserve a small sentinel cache fraction, compare each policy's shadow-predicted versus realized sentinel behavior to estimate a calibration residual, and use that residual to bias-correct whole-cache policy rankings before switching with simple hysteresis and a measured switch cost. Evaluate the method mainly in a CPU-only replay engine, then validate systems relevance with one small programmable-substrate result that switches between `LRU` and `MRU` on a real phase-changing workload.

related_work.title: Cache is King: Smart Page Eviction with eBPF
related_work.how_it_differs: Shows that Linux page-cache eviction policies can be customized safely with eBPF and that different policies matter, but it does not study online counterfactual ranking among multiple experts.

related_work.title: cache_ext: Customizing the Page Cache with eBPF
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides the main programmable Linux page-cache substrate for a small real-system validation, but assumes a selected policy rather than solving counterfactual ranking bias.

related_work.title: FetchBPF: Customizable Prefetching Policies in Linux with eBPF
related_work.how_it_differs: Demonstrates Linux memory-policy programmability for prefetching, not bias-corrected ranking of eviction experts.

related_work.title: P2Cache: An Application-Directed Page Cache for Improving Performance of Data-Intensive Applications
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides application-directed page-cache control and motivates policy specialization, but not online correction of sampled counterfactual bias under one shared cache.

related_work.title: PageFlex: Flexible and Efficient User-space Delegation of Linux Paging Policies with eBPF
related_work.how_it_differs: Shows low-overhead programmable Linux paging-policy delegation, but does not isolate biased counterfactual policy ranking in the page cache as the primary problem.

related_work.title: ACME: Adaptive Caching Using Multiple Experts
related_work.how_it_differs: Establishes adaptive expert selection as prior art; DuelCache is only novel if explicit bias correction improves ranking beyond generic expert adaptation.

related_work.title: SOPA: Selecting the Optimal Caching Policy Adaptively
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides the closest uncalibrated adaptive-selection baseline. DuelCache fails as a contribution if a SOPA-style recent-window selector matches it on the same sampled shadows.

related_work.title: Set-Dueling-Controlled Adaptive Insertion for High-Performance Caching
related_work.how_it_differs: Introduces leader-set style monitored regions. DuelCache uses a monitored region only as a calibration signal for biased whole-cache counterfactuals, not as the direct selector.

related_work.title: ARC: A Self-Tuning, Low Overhead Replacement Cache
related_work.how_it_differs: ARC is a strong adaptive single-policy baseline, but it does not rank multiple programmable Linux page-cache experts from counterfactual shadows.

hypothesis: Under one live shared Linux page cache, sampled inactive-policy shadows are biased enough that explicit sentinel-based bias correction improves expert-ranking accuracy and online miss cost over uncalibrated recent-window selection and direct leader-set dueling; if the same shadows rank well without correction or if sentinel residuals do not transfer to whole-cache error, the claim is false.

confirm: On at least two non-stationary workload families, DuelCache reduces weighted miss cost by at least 5% versus both `RecentWindow` and `LeaderSetDuel`.
On shortened exact replay windows, calibration improves Kendall tau by at least 0.10 absolute and reduces miss-cost prediction error by at least 15% versus `NoCalibration`.

The transfer study shows calibration helps in a majority of epochs for at least one balanced multi-tenant and one single-tenant phase-shift workload, while also identifying at least one concrete failure regime where transfer breaks down.
On `StationaryZipf`, DuelCache stays within 2% of the better of `LRU` and `ARC`.

In a small programmable-substrate experiment that switches between `LRU` and `MRU`, the controller improves either page-fault count or runtime by at least 3% over both static policies while keeping median switch latency below 250 ms.
refute: Exact replay shows little counterfactual ranking bias to correct under one active shared cache.
`RecentWindow` or `LeaderSetDuel` matches DuelCache within confidence intervals on the mixed-workload settings.
Sentinel residuals fail to transfer to full-cache ranking error except in narrow or contrived settings.
Calibration frequently worsens rankings under realistic tenant skew, sentinel placement, or phase volatility.
The real programmable-substrate run shows no end-to-end benefit or requires overhead large enough to make the mechanism implausible.
